title: Isaiah 27
subtitle: Leviathan Slain: The Isaiah Apocalypse Concludes

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — “In That Day the LORD Will Slay Leviathan”

heb.paragraph: "In that day, the LORD will punish with his sword — his fierce, great and powerful sword — Leviathan (liwyāṭān) the gliding serpent, Leviathan the coiling serpent; he will slay the monster of the sea" (v.1). The primordial chaos-monster of Job 41 and Psalm 74:14. In the Isaiah Apocalypse’s climax, God kills Leviathan. The serpent of Genesis 3, the chaos-dragon of creation mythology, the power behind the empires — slain. Then: "In that day: Sing about a fruitful vineyard!" (v.2). The vineyard of ch.5 (which produced bad fruit) is restored. "I, the LORD, watch over it; I water it continually. I guard it day and night" (v.3). God is no longer the destroying farmer (5:5–6) but the protecting gardener (27:3).

notes.note: "The LORD will slay Leviathan the gliding serpent, Leviathan the coiling serpent; he will slay the monster of the sea." The ultimate enemy destroyed. Revelation 12–13’s dragon draws on this: the ancient serpent, the chaos-monster, finally killed.

"Sing about a fruitful vineyard! I, the LORD, watch over it; I water it continually." The vineyard of ch.5 redeemed. What produced wild grapes now bears fruit. God’s gardening succeeds the second time.

"Israel will bud and blossom and fill all the world with fruit." The vineyard’s global harvest. Israel’s fruit fills the earth. The mission theology of the vineyard: planted locally, bearing fruit globally.

refs.note: God crushing Leviathan's heads — the same mythic-combat imagery.
'That ancient serpent called the devil' — Revelation's dragon draws on Isaiah's Leviathan.
Leviathan described in terrifying detail — only God can subdue this monster.
The dragon bound for a thousand years — the eschatological defeat of Leviathan.

hist.context: Leviathan (v.1) is the chaos-monster from Canaanite mythology, representing primordial evil and disorder. Isaiah demythologizes: Leviathan is not a rival god but a creature Yahweh will slay 'in that day.' The 'twisting serpent' and 'monster of the sea' may also represent hostile empires (Egypt, Babylon, Assyria). The vineyard song (vv.2-6) reverses chapter 5's judgment: instead of producing wild grapes, the vineyard is now fruitful, filling the world with fruit.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt sees Leviathan as representing all forces that oppose God's purposes — cosmic, political, and spiritual. The 'sword' (ḥereb) that slays the dragon is the same word used for Israel's enemies' destruction throughout Isaiah.
The vineyard reversal is crucial: chapter 5 ended with thorns and briers; chapter 27 has God removing thorns so the vineyard flourishes. Judgment served its purpose; now restoration can come.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs reads the Leviathan slaying as the climax of cosmic conflict. The 'Isaiah Apocalypse' began with earth's judgment (ch.24) and ends with chaos-monster's defeat (ch.27). The whole created order is liberated.
Childs notes the vineyard song functions as inclusio with chapter 5: what began in judgment concludes in restoration. The structure of Isaiah holds judgment and hope together.

sections.header: Verses 7–13 — “In That Day a Great Trumpet Will Sound”

heb.paragraph: "In that day a great trumpet (shōfār) will sound. Those who were perishing in Assyria and those who were exiled in Egypt will come and worship the LORD on the holy mountain in Jerusalem" (v.13). God’s discipline of Israel was measured: "By warfare and exile you contend with her — with his fierce blast he drives her out, as on a day the east wind blows" (v.8). The discipline produces atonement: "By this Jacob’s guilt is atoned for" (v.9). The altars will be demolished (v.9). Then the great ingathering: trumpet sounds, the scattered come from Assyria and Egypt, and worship in Jerusalem (v.13). The Isaiah Apocalypse ends with a trumpet and a pilgrimage.

notes.note: "By warfare and exile you contend with her. By this, Jacob’s guilt is atoned for." Exile as atonement. The suffering is not pointless but purgative. The altar-stones are crushed — idolatry finally ended.

"In that day the LORD will thresh from the Euphrates to the Wadi of Egypt. You will be gathered up one by one. A great trumpet will sound." The ingathering: individual, deliberate, trumpet-announced. Jesus echoes this in Matthew 24:31: "He will send his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect."

refs.note: The second gathering of the remnant from Assyria and Egypt — the same return theology.
Angels gathering the elect from the four winds — Jesus draws on Isaiah's trumpet imagery.
'At the last trumpet' — Paul's resurrection trumpet echoes Isaiah's gathering trumpet.
Survivors coming to Jerusalem to worship — the same pilgrimage eschatology.

hist.context: The 'great trumpet' (v.13) signals the ingathering of exiles from Assyria and Egypt — the two powers that swallowed Israel and Judah. This reverses the scattering of exile; the diaspora comes home. The 'altar in Jerusalem' (v.13) is the restored worship center. The punishment of Jacob (vv.7-9) was measured, not total — God distinguishes between discipline and destruction. The 'fortified city' (v.10) left desolate may be Jerusalem during exile or any proud city refusing God.

oswalt.source: John N. Oswalt, Isaiah, NICOT (2 vols., 1986/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Oswalt notes the distinction: Jacob's punishment is proportionate ('by measure,' v.8), not total destruction. The winnowing wind drives out impurity but preserves the grain. This is surgical judgment, not annihilation.
The agricultural metaphor continues: God will 'thresh' the grain from Euphrates to Egypt, gathering each kernel. The trumpet signals not battle but homecoming — the great ingathering.

childs.source: Brevard S. Childs, Isaiah, OTL (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Childs sees chapter 27 as the hinge of the book: the Isaiah Apocalypse concludes with restored Israel worshiping on the holy mountain. This points forward to chapters 40-66 where return and restoration dominate.
The 'great trumpet' became significant in Jewish liturgy — the shofar of Rosh Hashanah anticipates this final ingathering. Childs notes how worship appropriates prophetic hope.

ppl.text: "The LORD will slay Leviathan the gliding serpent, Leviathan the coiling serpent…

ppl.name: Israel
ppl.text: "Israel will bud and blossom and fill all the world with fruit…

ppl.name: Jacob
ppl.text: By this, Jacob’s guilt is atoned for…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVIn days to come Jacob will take root, Israel will bud and blossom and fill all the world with fruit.ESVIn days to come Jacob shall take root, Israel shall blossom and put forth shoots and fill the whole world with fruit.NIVI am not angry. If only there were briers and thorns confronting me! I would march against them in battle; I would set them all on fire.ESVI have no wrath. Would that I had thorns and briers to battle! I would march against them, I would burn them up together.

rec.text: ""The LORD will slay Leviathan the gliding serpent, Leviathan the coiling serpent; he will slay the monster of the sea." The ultimate enemy destroyed. Revelation 12–13’s dragon draws on this: the ancie…"

rows.label: The Great Trumpet — 27:7–13
rows.text: Exile as atonement. "A great TRUMPET will sound." Scattered gathered. Worship on the holy mountain. Matt 24:31.

hebtext.note: "In that day, the LORD will punish with his sword — his fierce, great and powerful sword — Leviathan (liwyāṭān) the gliding serpent, Leviathan the coiling serpent; he will slay the monster of the sea" (v.1). The primordial chaos-monster of Job 41 and Psalm 74:14. In the Isaiah Apocalypse’s climax, God kills Leviathan. The serpent of Genesis 3, the chaos-dragon of creation mythology, the power behind the empires — slain. Then: "In that day: Sing about a fruitful vineyard!" (v.2). The vineyard of ch.5 (which produced bad fruit) is restored. "I, the LORD, watch over it; I water it continually. I guard it day and night" (v.3). God is no longer the destroying farmer (5:5–6) but the protecting gardener (27:3).

"In that day a great trumpet (shōfār) will sound. Those who were perishing in Assyria and those who were exiled in Egypt will come and worship the LORD on the holy mountain in Jerusalem" (v.13). God’s discipline of Israel was measured: "By warfare and exile you contend with her — with his fierce blast he drives her out, as on a day the east wind blows" (v.8). The discipline produces atonement: "By this Jacob’s guilt is atoned for" (v.9). The altars will be demolished (v.9). Then the great ingathering: trumpet sounds, the scattered come from Assyria and Egypt, and worship in Jerusalem (v.13). The Isaiah Apocalypse ends with a trumpet and a pilgrimage.

themes.note: Sovereignty and Prophecy peak: Leviathan slain (Rev 12–13), vineyard redeemed, great trumpet (Matt 24:31). The Isaiah Apocalypse concludes: chaos-monster dead, vineyard fruitful, scattered gathered, worship restored.

coaching.tip: Leviathan (v. 1) — the chaos sea-monster from Canaanite mythology — appears here as a figure of cosmic evil that God will slay 'in that day.' The same creature appears in Job 41 and Psalm 74:14. By using mythological imagery, Isaiah isn't endorsing pagan religion but commandeering its vocabulary: whatever chaos you fear, Yahweh is bigger. Revelation 12–13's beast carries this thread to completion.

'In days to come Jacob will take root, Israel will bud and blossom and fill all the world with fruit' (v. 6). The vineyard image from chapter 5 returns — but transformed. The vineyard that produced wild grapes will now fill the earth with fruit. Isaiah revisits his own earlier images and redeems them. Literary self-correction becomes theological hope.
